questions.prompt: A correlation coefficient of r = −0.85 between hours of TV and exam scores indicates:
choices: a computational error, since r cannot be negative

questions.prompt: Which value of r indicates the STRONGEST linear relationship?
Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are strongly correlated. The best explanation is:
choices: coincidence — with enough data everything correlates
a lurking variable (summer weather) drives both

questions.prompt: A perfect U-shaped relationship between two variables would produce a correlation coefficient near:
A bar chart's vertical axis starts at 96 instead of 0, making a 2% difference between bars look enormous. This technique is called:
A treatment looks worse than a control overall, yet better within both the mild-case and severe-case subgroups. This reversal is known as:

A study finds r = 0.72 between the number of firefighters sent to a fire and the damage the fire causes. A politician concludes that sending fewer firefighters would reduce damage. Explain the error and identify the lurking variable.
questions.modelAnswer: The politician has read a correlation as a cause. Fire severity is the lurking variable: bigger fires get more firefighters AND cause more damage, so the two rise together without one causing the other. Cutting firefighters would not shrink fires — the correlation reflects the severity driving both variables.

rubric.description: Identifies the mistake as treating correlation as causation
Names fire size/severity as the lurking variable driving both counts

questions.prompt: Six data points show a clean linear pattern with r ≈ 0.99. One additional unusual point drops r to 0.1. What property of r does this demonstrate, and what should you always do before trusting a reported correlation?
questions.modelAnswer: It demonstrates that r is not resistant: a single outlier can destroy (or manufacture) a correlation, because r is built from means and deviations that extreme points dominate. Before trusting any reported r, look at the scatterplot — the plot reveals outliers, curvature, and clusters that the single number hides.

rubric.description: Names non-resistance of r to outliers
Prescribes examining the scatterplot before trusting r

questions.prompt: A headline reads: 'Shark attacks double at local beaches!' What single follow-up question from this lesson's field guide should you ask first, and why does it matter?
questions.modelAnswer: Ask for the actual counts — the silent denominator. Doubling from one attack to two is a very different story than from 50 to 100, and percentage-change language hides the base. Rates and changes only mean something once you know the raw numbers they were computed from.

rubric.description: Asks about the base counts / denominator behind the percentage change
Explains that small bases make dramatic-sounding relative changes trivial

questions.prompt: You compute r = 0.45 between daily study minutes and quiz scores for your class. Write two honest sentences reporting this result: one stating what the data shows, and one stating what it does NOT show.
questions.modelAnswer: The data shows a moderate positive linear association between study minutes and quiz scores in this class — students who studied more tended to score somewhat higher. It does not show that studying caused the higher scores: motivated students may both study more and score higher, and this observational data cannot separate those explanations.

rubric.description: Reports a moderate positive association, correctly hedged to this data
Explicitly disclaims causation and ideally names a plausible lurking factor
